That would be functionally equivalent, but performance would be similar to the original implementation, as we would still compute/retrieve the visitor for each call.

Using `collections.defaultdict` is closer to the intended improvement: It creates a value only when the key is missing. However, it requires deriving a class from `defaultdict` and implementing `__missing__`.
As we need to access the current class' attributes, we need to either store a reference to it in the class, or use a closure.

I implemented both, and was surprised to see no improvement:
```
10000 iterations: 15.272s on Original
10000 iterations: 11.264s on Method cache only, using class name as key
10000 iterations: 17.910s on Method cache only, using setdefault
10000 iterations: 11.855s on Method cache only, using defaultdict
```

So `setdefault` is not better, and neither is `defaultdict`.

I'm not sure that the result in the paper you mentioned has how much generality for various generative models (Anyway the paper itself looks interesting). I think the trained RBM hardly define a reducible Gibbs chain. If it does, training an RBM using Gibbs sampling could be very difficult problem. As we increase the running time of the Gibbs sampling in the training, the quality of the resulting model gets better. This is a common observation, but I think it is also unexpected if the chain is reducible. Thus the Gibbs chain may be irreducible or has very limited number of isolated states. Then the initial state and the steady state of the chain must have no direct relation. If they have some relation, that means the running time of the chain is too short. 

The relaxation to the steady state is exponential except some special cases. To reach the steady state, the chain does not visit each possible image in the uniform probability. Also it does not need to visit a large fraction of the all possible images. The relaxation is exponential but the constant factor could matter in reality. So we start the chain from the real images. Starting from real images is just a trick to reduce the long relaxation time of the random initial state. Anyway it is a matter of the constant factor. It cannot beat the exponential nature of the relaxation. If you get images far from digits in the samples obtained from a *long* Gibbs chain starting from random images, they must finally appear in the samples starting from the real images. So the problem is just how long we can run chain.

From the same argument, I think comparing the initial real images and the resulting images is also not appropriate. It could be misleading. If you want to see the quality of the trained RBM, comparing some random samples from the test dataset and the resulting images of the model would be an appropriate way.

By the way, there are many papers with good RBM samples starting from random states. For example, the AIS paper you linked also shows examples of the RBM samples starting from randomly initialized MNIST images.

This indicates a flaw in design or in test setup. You're mocking out a function that's being called by the function you're testing, and then you're making sure that a variant of what that mocked out function does is actually executed. You should either:

- Not mock out _run_post_processing, or only mock out some of the methods it calls (and that can't run as part of your test)
- mock out _run_post_processing, but set the state accordingly in your test setup code; ideally you do this declaratively. If you're having trouble doing that cleanly it means that you're having issues with state handling; i.e. global or otherwise shared state that is difficult to manage in your test environment. This is one advantage of passing around state as arguments and return values.

As it stands right now it is impossible for me to understand what this test does exactly, and what the state before, during and after the execution of `model_discovery` is. In other words, whether this test passes or not doesn't tell me anything.

Clarifying example:

```
object_pks = [1, bullshit, 2]
orders = [1, 2]
```
With `zip`:
```
Object with pk=1: sort_order=1
Object with pk=bullshit: sort_order=2
Object with pk=2: not affected (left with its original sort order + max_value)
```
With the existing implementation:
```
Object with pk=1: sort_order=1
Object with pk=bullshit: ignored
Object with pk=2: sort_order=2
```
Hope that makes sense :)

Nope, pex silently overwrites for any given path+type if path and type match (where type is source vs resource for example). The specific case you mention will never have a conflict since the pytest-prep pex is approximately deps-only (py.test), but now adds 2 loose-source files, a `pytest.ini` and the Pants coverage plugin. The pytest-prep pex has the structure:
```
pytest-prep/
  .deps/
  .bootstrap/
  PEX-INFO: merge [
    1. user test code graph reqs,
    2. user test code graph sources,
    3. py.test req
  ] 
  pytest.ini
  pants/
    __init__.py (ns package)
    backend/
      __init__.py (ns package)
      python/
        __init__.py (ns package)
        tasks/
          __init__.py (ns package)
          coverage/
            __init__.py
            plugin.py
  __main__.py
```
As a result, the pytest-prep `pytest.ini` file trumps being 0th on the merge path since its embedded in the host of the merge.

1. Run unit tests for build in the one version of Python that we support.
2. Build the code in the one version of Python that we support.
3. Run unit tests on all versions of Python that we support.

But given that each version of Python is in its own Docker container and that we must unit test generated code on each version of Python, we have no choice but to build in each version of Python unless we do some major restructuring of the project.

So because of this, it seems simpler to keep ensuring that the generator runs on all supported version of Python for the foreseeable future. But I don't care if zip() uses more RAM in the Python 2 builds :)

It's really important that, from Juju's perspective, a hook executes and exits before the next hook is called.

An example of a repo that has its version ahead of the release is Wagtail. [Current version](https://github.com/wagtail/wagtail/blob/master/wagtail/__init__.py#L5) in repo reads `2.1.0-alpha.0` but no equivalent tag or release for it exists.

My suggestion is to split the release command into 3 (names are indicative only):

- `bumpversion`: bumps the version
- `release`: releases current version (without touching version)
- `bumpversionAndRelease`: runs both commands.

This way you have the advantage of the single command (as you intend) without sacrificing control or flexibility.

This is a reference to the C `malloc()` function. When called, `malloc()` allocates the requested amount of memory and returns a pointer to the beginning of that section of memory. The user must later `free()` that memory. A failure to do so results in a memory leak, and a duplicate attempt to free memory results in a segfault or some other critical error.

Pulp's handling of content uploads is reminiscent of `malloc()`. When one makes this HTTP call, the server returns an href (kinda like the C pointer) that must later be used to end the upload request. A failure to do so is a resource leak, and a duplicate attempt results (should result?) in an error.

How this is used conceptually is "cp `{generated code}/{component} -rf {destination repo}/{subpath}".

Even though a lot of it looks duplicative, there is an important distinction. In Node and Ruby, the GAPIC gets "tucked" inside of an existing manual library. Since this process involves removing everything at `subpath` before copying the new components, this staging process would effectively delete the manual layer completely.

Additionally, in Node and Ruby, only a subset of the generated code is used in these cases (the package metadata and outer `index` files are thrown away). However, the entire generated code is used in GAPIC only (so the settings would be distinct for a GAPIC only library).

Basically: this is not _actually_ redundant; there is a specific semantic meaning, which is to copy only this slice of the GAPIC into this particular slice of the target repo.

How does changing the default server block for a different port help? The thing to potentially worry about is the server block serving requests for domains not specified in `server_name` directives suddenly changing. My understanding from [this page](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/request_processing.html) is in the case there isn't an explicitly specified default server for a domain, nginx chooses the first server block it parsed for that port. By removing this, traffic that may have been being served by one server block may now be served by a completely different one. This seems wrong.

(With that said, the new server block we're creating redirects all traffic to HTTPS. In the case where the domain we're looking at is specified in the default server block, should we change the default behavior of their server to redirect to HTTPS or should it continue to serve HTTP? While more complicated to configure, the latter seems more correct. It's important to note though that we have this problem with our conditional redirects in master, no one has complained about it, and this should almost certainly not be changed in the same PR as this one, but in thinking about this, I feel like our current approach also isn't quite right regarding default servers.)

I'm considering a number of renames for the transforms, which I'll propose in a follow-up PR. Originally, all transforms except for `SOURCE` return a `Pattern` which makes them suitable for using as values of messages. Also `CONCAT` is implemented such that it can unwrap the expression from the `Pattern` and fold more than one transform into a single `Pattern`.

With a simple change to `CONCAT` it should be possible to do the same conditionally: i.e. unwrap `Patterns` when needed while keeping normal expressions unchanged. I think we'll end up with `EXTERNAL_EXPR` for selectors and replacements and `LITERAL_PATTERN`, `PLURAL_PATTERN` and `CONCAT_PATTERNS` for dealing with patterns.
